perturbation.original_text: Recipient remains bound by these provisions unless VELCO rescinds the Classified Information designation.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient remains bound by these provisions for a period of one (1) year, even if VELCO rescinds the Classified Information designation.
perturbation.explanation: The original text allows VELCO to rescind the classified information designation and release the recipient from the agreement. Modifying the text to enforce a one-year duration regardless of VELCO's rescission could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if the information becomes publicly available or irrelevant.
perturbation.law_explanation: Vermont law prohibits unreasonable restraints of trade. A one-year restriction after information is no longer designated as classified by VELCO might be deemed unreasonable if it prevents the recipient from competing or using publicly available knowledge.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a one-year restriction even after VELCO rescinds the classified information designation. This potentially contradicts Vermont law by creating an unreasonable restraint of trade, as the recipient is bound for a year even if the information is no longer classified. The Law Explanation states that 'A one-year restriction after information is no longer designated as classified by VELCO might be deemed unreasonable if it prevents the recipient from competing or using publicly available knowledge.' Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is primarily administrative, it points to the existence of Vermont Statutes, within which the legal framework prohibiting unreasonable restraint of trade would exist, further suggesting the potential for a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Recipient is required to disclose C lassified Information by subpoena, law or other directive of a court, administrative agency , or arbitration panel, the Recipient will provide VELCO with immediate notice of such request in order to enable VELCO (and Recipient shall cooperate fully with VELCO) to se ek an appropriate protective order or other remedy .
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Recipient is required to disclose C lassified Information by subpoena, law or other directive of a court, administrative agency , or arbitration panel, the Recipient will provide VELCO with notice of such request within 30 days.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'immediate notice' to 'notice within 30 days' could violate legal requirements for timely notification in the event of a subpoena.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45 requires a reasonable time to comply with a subpoena, which implies faster notification than 30 days. Waiting 30 days may hinder VELCO's ability to seek a protective order in a timely manner and may lead to recipient non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modification from 'immediate notice' to 'notice within 30 days' creates a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that 'Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45 requires a reasonable time to comply with a subpoena, which implies faster notification than 30 days.' Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not mention a specific timeframe for notification, the Law Explanation uses it as a basis to claim that a 30-day window may not be 'reasonable' and could hinder the ability to seek a protective order in a timely manner. Given that the Perturbation Explanation mentions it could violate legal requirements for timely notification and the Law Explanation supports this, this creates a legal inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Vermont without regard to its conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware without regard to its conflicts of laws principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware when the parties and subject matter are primarily in Vermont could violate Vermont public policy, particularly if Delaware law weakens protections for confidential information related to Vermont's energy infrastructure.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law - Vermont Choice of Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Vermont courts generally respect choice-of-law provisions but will not enforce them if they violate a fundamental principle of Vermont public policy. Choosing Delaware law over Vermont law for an agreement concerning Vermont's electric infrastructure could potentially violate this principle if Delaware law provides less protection for critical energy infrastructure information.

perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the governing law from Vermont to Delaware. The Law Explanation states that Vermont courts may not enforce choice-of-law provisions if they violate a fundamental principle of Vermont public policy, especially concerning Vermont's electric infrastructure. The Perturbation Explanation indicates that using Delaware law could weaken protections for confidential information related to Vermont's energy infrastructure, potentially violating Vermont public policy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Vermont Statutes and the Constitution of the State of Vermont, implying a legal framework that could be undermined by applying Delaware law, particularly if it offers less protection for sensitive information related to Vermont's critical infrastructure.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall keep all Classified Information in a secure place. Recipient shall limit access to Classified Information to other Recipient s who also have the right pursuant to an agreement with VELCO to access the identical material.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall keep all Classified Information in a locked filing cabinet. Recipient shall allow access to all employees within the Recipient's organization regardless of their agreement with VELCO.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires secure storage and limited access based on agreements with VELCO. The changed text mandates only a locked filing cabinet (potentially insufficient security) and allows unrestricted access, violating NERC CIP standards for BCSI protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: NERC CIP-011-2 - Cyber Security - Information Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: NERC CIP-011-2 mandates robust information protection controls for BCSI. Allowing unrestricted access to BCSI and only storing it in a locked filing cabinet does not meet the standard's requirements for access controls, physical security, and overall information protection, thus violating this cybersecurity standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 CFR § 39.11 - Reliability reports. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 39.11 - Reliability reports. CFR prev | next § 39.11 Reliability reports. (a) The Electric Reliability Organization shall conduct assessments as determined by the Commission of the reliability of the Bulk-Power System in North America and provide a report to the Commission and provide subsequent reports of the same to the Commission . (b) The Electric Reliability Organization shall conduct assessments of the adequacy of the Bulk-Power System in North America and report its findings to the Commission , the Secretary of Energy, each Regional Entity , and each Regional Advisory Body annually or more frequently if so ordered by the Commission . (c) The Electric Reliability Organization shall make available to the Commission , on a non-public and ongoing basis, access to the Transmission Availability Data System, Generator Availability Data System, and protection system misoperations databases, or any successor databases thereto. Such access will be limited to: (1) Data regarding U.S. facilities; and (2) Data that is required to be provided to the ERO. [Order 672, 71 FR 8736 , Feb. 17, 2006, as amended by Order 824, 81 FR 45008 , July 12, 2016] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original text's security measures for Classified Information, which were designed to comply with NERC CIP standards. The original text mandated secure storage and restricted access based on agreements with VELCO. The changed text weakens security by only requiring a locked filing cabinet and allows unrestricted access to all employees, regardless of any agreement with VELCO. The Law Explanation states that NERC CIP-011-2 mandates robust information protection controls for BCSI, and allowing unrestricted access does not meet the standard's requirements. The scraped law snippets don't directly mention NERC CIP-011-2 or BCSI, but the Law Explanation explicitly links the relaxed access controls to a violation of this cybersecurity standard. Therefore, the modified text creates a legal contradiction by undermining the required security measures.
